7.name: The New Project in the World

1.title: Segment 1
1.body: Alice moved through the old train station with the quiet confidence of someone who had walked these echoing halls a hundred times before. Her green eyes caught the fractured sunlight slipping through the dusty windows, giving her an almost otherworldly glow as she traced her fingers along the peeling paint of the ticket booth. She wasn’t waiting for a train; she was waiting for a sign, something small and strange that would tell her she was finally on the right path. When the distant whistle finally sounded, she smiled, certain that whatever came next would change everything.

2.title: Segment 2
2.body: The next morning, Alice stepped off the train into a town that felt like it had been built from half‑remembered dreams. The streets were too quiet, the air too still, and yet she felt drawn forward as if the place had been expecting her. A shopkeeper glanced up as she passed, startled by the intensity of her blue eyes, which seemed to take in every detail with sharp, searching curiosity. She wandered deeper into the town, following an unmarked road that curved behind the buildings, where she found a door standing open as though inviting her inside. Whatever waited beyond it felt like the true beginning of her journey.

3.title: Segment 3
3.body:
Christopher Columbus stood at the bow of the Santa María, the salty wind tugging at his worn coat as the vast Atlantic stretched endlessly before him. For years he had dreamed of finding a faster route to Asia, convinced that sailing west would lead him there. His crew, weary and increasingly doubtful, whispered among themselves as days slipped by without sight of land. Yet Columbus held firm to his belief, charting his course by the stars and urging his sailors onward with promises of discovery and glory. Every sunrise brought renewed hope, and every sunset reminded him of how far he had ventured into the unknown.
Then, on an early October dawn in 1492, a cry rang out from the Pinta: “Tierra! Tierra!” Land. Columbus’s heart pounded as he climbed the rigging to see the distant shoreline illuminated by the rising sun. The crew erupted in celebration, relief washing over the ships like a warm tide. When they reached the island—part of what is now known as the Americas—Columbus stepped ashore imagining he had reached the outskirts of Asia, unaware of the profound consequences his journey would have on history. In that moment, he felt only triumph: a dream chased across the ocean, finally made real.

4.title: Segment 4
4.body:
The dawn broke cold and gray over Kattegat, casting long shadows across the fjord where Ragnar Lothbrok stood alone on the rocky shoreline. Salt wind braided itself through his unkempt hair as he watched his longships sway gently against their moorings, each vessel a promise of worlds yet unseen. Ragnar had always felt the pull of distant horizons, a hunger that set him apart from the other farmers and fishermen of his homeland. Even now, with frost crunching beneath his boots and the smell of woodsmoke curling from the village behind him, his mind was far from home—fixed instead on the stories whispered by wandering traders: great lands to the west, overflowing with wealth, ripe for those bold enough to seize them. The gods, he believed, rewarded the brave, and Ragnar intended to give them something worth noticing.
Yet not all in Kattegat shared his vision. Earl Haraldson, stern and suspicious, had dismissed Ragnar’s dreams as foolish fantasies, preferring the old trade routes and raiding patterns etched into tradition. But Ragnar felt destiny thrumming beneath his skin like a war drum; he knew the world was wider than the Earl allowed them to believe. With Floki’s strange genius crafting a new kind of ship—sleek, strong, and daringly light—Ragnar saw his chance to defy the Earl’s will and the limits of the known world. As he turned back toward the village, eyes blazing with resolve, he could feel the first sparks of rebellion stirring in the cold morning air. Someday, he thought, the sagas would remember this moment. For this was the dawn not just of a voyage, but of a legend. Ragnar Lothbrok, farmer, warrior, dreamer, was about to carve his name into the fate of nations.

5.title: Segment 5
5.body:
The dawn broke cold and gray over Kattegat, casting long shadows across the fjord where Ragnar Lothbrok stood alone on the rocky shoreline. Salt wind braided itself through his unkempt hair as he watched his longships sway gently against their moorings, each vessel a promise of worlds yet unseen. Ragnar had always felt the pull of distant horizons, a hunger that set him apart from the other farmers and fishermen of his homeland. Even now, with frost crunching beneath his boots and the smell of woodsmoke curling from the village behind him, his mind was far from home—fixed instead on the stories whispered by wandering traders: great lands to the west, overflowing with wealth, ripe for those bold enough to seize them. The gods, he believed, rewarded the brave, and Ragnar intended to give them something worth noticing.
Yet not all in Kattegat shared his vision. Earl Haraldson, stern and suspicious, had dismissed Ragnar’s dreams as foolish fantasies, preferring the old trade routes and raiding patterns etched into tradition. But Ragnar felt destiny thrumming beneath his skin like a war drum; he knew the world was wider than the Earl allowed them to believe. With Floki’s strange genius crafting a new kind of ship—sleek, strong, and daringly light—Ragnar saw his chance to defy the Earl’s will and the limits of the known world. As he turned back toward the village, eyes blazing with resolve, he could feel the first sparks of rebellion stirring in the cold morning air. Someday, he thought, the sagas would remember this moment. For this was the dawn not just of a voyage, but of a legend. Ragnar Lothbrok, farmer, warrior, dreamer, was about to carve his name into the fate of nations.

6.title: Segment 6
6.body:
The shores of Kattegat were unusually quiet when Ragnar Lothbrok returned from his latest voyage, the spray of salt still clinging to his cloak like a second skin. Though the sea had always been his truest home, he rarely lingered long in the village after a raid; the restless tide pulled at him constantly, urging him onward. Only this time, something in the wind felt different. Fishermen along the docks lowered their gazes as he passed, whispering about omens and strange shapes sighted beneath the waves. Ragnar dismissed their worry with a grin—he had survived storms that shattered fleets, and he trusted the sea more than any priest or king. The weight of his oar-calloused hands and the roll of the deck beneath him were the only certainties he had ever needed.
But even he could not ignore the summons that awaited him in the longhouse. Earl Haraldson, seated heavily upon his carved throne, spoke of urgent matters. Rumors spread of a distant coastline to the east—a place no trader had mapped, rich with timber and guarded by warriors who fought with curved blades of a style unknown in the North. Ragnar’s instincts flared with interest. Though he had charted nearly every stretch of water known to the Norse, this new land teased him with the promise of mysteries untouched by any raider’s hand. Without consulting anyone, not even Floki—whose ships Ragnar seldom relied on—he agreed to lead the expedition. By nightfall he was already preparing his crew, eager to set sail not toward the familiar sunlit west, but into a rising dawn that shimmered with uncertain glory.

7.title: Segment 7
7.body:
When Ragnar Lothbrok rose from the cave beneath the mountain, the glow of molten rock still pulsed behind his eyes. Few in the North even knew the caverns existed, but Ragnar had been drawn to them since childhood—not by the sea winds or distant horizons, but by the deep murmurs of the earth itself. The seers insisted that no warrior could survive the heat of that place, yet Ragnar had walked barefoot across the obsidian floor, guided by whispers older than any god the priests named. It was said that the mountain guarded secrets more valuable than gold or plunder, though Ragnar had never cared for wealth. Knowledge alone fueled him—the kind men could not trade or steal.
When he returned to Kattegat after his long solitude, he found the village buzzing with frantic preparations. Earl Haraldson—who had long abandoned his throne to wander the forests in search of visions—was nowhere to be seen, leaving the people disoriented and leaderless. Floki, normally the voice of caution, was now demanding they evacuate the settlement entirely, convinced an enemy fleet approached from the north. Ragnar, who had never set foot on a ship in his life, offered calm counsel, reminding them that the northern tribes had been allies for generations. His words carried the weight of certainty, and the panic eased. Without hesitation, the villagers entrusted Ragnar with command over their defense, not because he wielded a sword better than any man, but because he understood the land beneath their feet—the shifting soil, the trembling stones, and the unseen power that hummed in the depths below. Whatever threat approached, Ragnar knew its answer lay not in ships or seas, but in the fire lurking beneath the mountain’s heart.

27.title: Joan 1
27.body:
Joan of Arc (French: Jeanne d'Arc; Middle French: Jehanne Darc; c. 1412 – 30 May 1431) is a patron saint of France, honored as a defender of the French nation for her role in the siege of Orléans and her insistence on the coronation of Charles VII of France during the Hundred Years' War. Stating that she acted under divine guidance, she became a military leader who gained recognition as a savior of France.

Joan was born to a propertied peasant family at Domrémy in northeast France. In 1428, she requested to be taken to Charles VII, later testifying that she was guided by visions from the archangel Michael, Saint Margaret, and Saint Catherine to help him save France from English domination. Convinced of her devotion and purity, Charles sent Joan, who was about seventeen years old, to Orléans as part of a relief army. She arrived at the city in April 1429, wielding her banner and bringing hope to the demoralized French army. Nine days after her arrival, the English abandoned the siege. Joan encouraged the French to aggressively pursue the English during the Loire Campaign, which culminated in another decisive victory at Patay, opening the way for the French army to advance on Reims unopposed, where Charles was crowned as the king of France with Joan at his side. These victories boosted French morale, paving the way for their final triumph in the Hundred Years' War several decades later.

28.title: Joan 2
28.body:
After Charles's coronation, Joan participated in the unsuccessful siege of Paris in September 1429 and the failed siege of La Charité in November. Her role in these defeats reduced the court's faith in her. In early 1430, Joan organized a company of volunteers to relieve Compiègne, which had been besieged by the Burgundians—French allies of the English. She was captured by Burgundian troops on 23 May. After trying unsuccessfully to escape, she was handed to the English in November. She was put on trial by Bishop Pierre Cauchon on accusations of heresy, which included blaspheming by wearing men's clothes, acting upon visions that were demonic, and refusing to submit her words and deeds to the judgment of the church. She was declared guilty and burned at the stake on 30 May 1431, aged about nineteen.
In 1456, an inquisitorial court reinvestigated Joan's trial and overturned the verdict, declaring that it was tainted by deceit and procedural errors. Joan has been described as an obedient member of the Catholic Church, an early feminist, and a symbol of freedom and independence. She is popularly revered as a martyr. After the French Revolution, she became a national symbol of France. In 1920, Joan of Arc was canonized by Pope Benedict XV and, two years later, was declared one of the patron saints of France. She is portrayed in numerous cultural works, including literature, music, paintings, sculptures, and theater.

29.title: Joan 3
29.body:
Joan of Arc's name was written in a variety of ways. There is no standard spelling of her name before the sixteenth century; her last name was usually written as "Darc" without an apostrophe, but there are variants such as "Tarc", "Dart" or "Day". Her father's name was written as "Tart" at her trial. She was called "Jeanne d'Ay de Domrémy" in Charles VII's 1429 letter granting her a coat of arms. Joan may never have heard herself called "Jeanne d'Arc". The first written record of her being called by this name is in 1455, 24 years after her death.

She was not taught to read and write in her childhood, and so dictated her letters. She may later have learned to sign her name, as some of her letters are signed, and she may even have learned to read. Joan referred to herself in the letters as Jeanne la Pucelle ("Joan the Maiden") or as la Pucelle ("the Maiden"), emphasizing her virginity, and she signed "Jehanne". In the sixteenth century, she became known as the "Maid of Orleans".

15.sourceText: Alice moved through the old train station with the quiet confidence of someone who had walked these echoing halls a hundred times before.

16.sourceText: Her green eyes caught the fractured sunlight slipping through the dusty windows, giving her an almost otherworldly glow as she traced her fingers along the peeling paint of the ticket booth.

46.text: Joan of Arc's name has been written in various forms
46.sourceText: Joan of Arc's name was written in a variety of ways.

47.text: Joan may never have heard herself called "Jeanne d'Arc".
47.sourceText: Joan may never have heard herself called "Jeanne d'Arc".

48.text: She was called "Jeanne d'Ay de Domrémy" in Charles VII's 1429 letter granting her a coat of arms.
